if len(history) > 0:
    init_seg = history[0].frame.segmentation
    print("Initial state nodes:")

            b = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)} cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

But we only have 4 red blocks (2 attached to character + 2 near frame). And the interior is 15 x 3 = 45 cells. 4 blocks x 9 cells = 36 cells. Not enough to fill the interior.

If the wall is 3 cells thick:
- Outer: 21 x 9 = 189 cells
- Inner: (21-6) x (9-6) = 15 x 3 = 45 cells
- Wall: 189 - 45 = 144 cells. That matches!
So the frame has 3-cell thick walls, and the interior is 15 x 3 = 45 cells.
The red blocks are 3x3 each. The interior is 15 x 3. So 5 blocks (15/3 = 5) can fit vertically in the interior.
We have 4 red blocks. If we move all 4 into the interior, they'd occupy 4 x 9 = 36 cells, leaving 9 cells empty.
But wait, maybe the character also needs to be positioned inside. The character is 135 pixels. If the character's "body" (the U-shape) fits inside the frame, it would cover the interior.

- Red blocks id=7,8 at rows 35-37, 41-43 (near the frame)
The character is a U-shape opening upward. The red blocks below it are at the "feet" of the U.
